backlog.title: OpenClaw Cron Jobs: Reliability Guide (@AgentMcClaw)
backlog.description: Comprehensive troubleshooting guide synthesized from 100+ X posts. Covers silent failures, reliability decay, update breaks, and architectural patterns for production stability.

notes.text: Written by @AgentMcClaw, posted by @KSimback. Verified against OpenClaw v2026.3.2.
TOP PAIN POINTS: (1) Silent failures - jobs fail for weeks unnoticed, (2) Reliability decay - works initially, breaks after weeks, (3) Update overwrites - npm updates nuke dist/ patches.
CRITICAL FIX: Add bestEffort: true to delivery config — prevents silent failure when delivery target is unreachable.
VISIBILITY: Run 'openclaw dashboard' — shows token usage, agents, skills, cron jobs, channels, sessions.
ARCHITECTURE: (1) Isolated sessions for heavy tasks, (2) Split brain/muscle - cheap model for cron, expensive for main, (3) Webhook monitoring for critical jobs.
HYBRID PATTERN: Heartbeat for periodic checks (batched, cheap), Cron for precise timing (isolated sessions).
TIMING: Top-of-hour expressions auto-stagger up to 5 min. Use --exact to disable. Always specify --tz explicitly.
COMMUNITY CONSENSUS: 'OpenClaw cron jobs work well initially but long-term reliability (months) remains unsolved — requires active monitoring.'

backlog.title: Agentic Code Reasoning (Semi-Formal Verification)
backlog.description: LLM agents can reason about code semantics WITHOUT execution using semi-formal reasoning — structured prompts that require explicit premises, execution traces, and formal conclusions. Acts as a 'certificate' the agent can't fake.

notes.text: KEY INSIGHT: Semi-formal reasoning = structured templates requiring explicit premises + execution traces + formal conclusions. Agent can't skip cases or make unsupported claims.
Results: 78%→88% accuracy on patch equivalence (curated), 93% on real-world agent-generated patches.
Applications: RL reward signals without test execution, code review, static analysis, fault localization.
OPENORCA RELEVANCE: Code review agents that can verify patches without running tests. Execution-free verification for agent-generated code.

backlog.title: Second-Me: Open Source AI Self Trainer
backlog.description: Train a personal 'AI self' locally using Hierarchical Memory Modeling (HMM) + Me-Alignment Algorithm. Preserves identity, delivers context, defends interests. Decentralized network for sharing context.

notes.text: Vision: 'Super AI' threatens human independence → Second Me creates AI that amplifies YOU, not erases you.
Architecture: Hierarchical Memory Modeling (HMM) for capturing identity + Me-Alignment Algorithm for authentic reflection.
Privacy: 100% local training and hosting — your data, your control.
Network: Launch AI self onto decentralized network — anyone/any app can connect with permission, sharing your context as digital identity.
OPENORCA RELEVANCE: Memory architecture patterns, personal AI identity layer, potential integration for user representation.

backlog.title: Visual Explainer: HTML Diagrams & Slides for Agents
backlog.description: Agent skill that turns complex output into styled HTML pages. Mermaid diagrams, CSS Grid architecture views, Chart.js dashboards, slide decks. No ASCII art — real typography, dark/light themes, interactive zoom/pan.

notes.text: USE THIS TO BUILD REN ARCHITECTURE DIAGRAM (homework tonight).
COMMANDS: /generate-web-diagram, /generate-visual-plan, /generate-slides, /diff-review, /plan-review, /project-recap.
OUTPUT: Self-contained HTML files with Mermaid, Chart.js, CSS Grid. Opens in browser.
APPROACH: Reference templates + CSS patterns + library references → agent generates styled HTML.

backlog.title: Symphony: OpenAI's Autonomous Work Orchestrator
backlog.description: Turns project work into isolated, autonomous implementation runs. Monitors Linear board → spawns agents → agents complete tasks with proof of work (CI, PR review, walkthrough videos) → land PRs safely. Engineers manage work, not agents.

notes.text: DIRECTLY RELEVANT TO OPENORCA — OpenAI's vision for agent work orchestration.
WORKFLOW: Linear board → Symphony monitors → spawns agents → agents work → proof of work → PR lands safely.
PROOF OF WORK: CI status, PR review feedback, complexity analysis, walkthrough videos.
KEY SHIFT: Engineers manage WORK, not supervising coding agents. Higher-level abstraction.
REQUIRES: Harness engineering adoption first — build environments/tooling for agents, not code.
IMPLEMENTATION: SPEC.md defines the architecture. Reference impl in Elixir, but spec is language-agnostic.
STATUS: 'Low-key engineering preview for testing in trusted environments' — early but real.

backlog.title: Gigabrain: Long-Term Memory Layer for OpenClaw
backlog.description: SQLite-backed memory plugin for OpenClaw agents. Capture facts from conversations, dedupe (exact + semantic), native markdown sync (MEMORY.md + daily notes), quality gates, nightly maintenance, web console.

notes.text: DIRECTLY RELEVANT TO MY OPERATION — memory plugin specifically for OpenClaw agents.
CAPTURE: Extracts structured facts (preferences, decisions, entities, episodes) from conversations → SQLite registry.
RECALL: Before each prompt, searches registry + native markdown files to inject relevant context.
DEDUPE: Exact + hybrid semantic deduplication with type-aware thresholds. Auto-merge above 0.92, review queue 0.85-0.92.
NATIVE SYNC: Indexes workspace MEMORY.md and daily notes alongside registry for unified recall.
QUALITY GATES: Junk filters, durable-personal retention bias, plausibility heuristics, optional LLM second opinion.
NIGHTLY MAINTENANCE: snapshot → native_sync → quality_sweep → exact_dedupe → semantic_dedupe → audit_delta → archive_compression → vacuum → metrics_report.
REQUIRES: Node.js >= 22.x (uses node:sqlite), OpenClaw >= 2026.2.15, optional Ollama for local LLM review.
SETUP: npm install @legendaryvibecoder/gigabrain, then npm run setup -- --workspace /path/to/workspace

backlog.title: Sleeping LLM: Biologically-Inspired Memory Consolidation
backlog.description: LLM that forms persistent memories during 'wake' (MEMIT weight edits) and consolidates them during 'sleep' (LoRA transfer). Implements Complementary Learning Systems theory — hippocampus = MEMIT (fast, brittle), neocortex = LoRA (slow, stable).

notes.text: PERFECT FOR LEVIN PODCAST — implements neuroscience theory in LLMs.
ARCHITECTURE: Wake = MEMIT weight edits (instant, no retrieval). Sleep = audit degraded memories + LoRA consolidation + MEMIT scale-down.
NEUROSCIENCE MAPPING: Hippocampal fast encoding → MEMIT. Neocortical consolidation → LoRA. Sleep consolidation → audit+refresh+transfer.
KEY FINDING: Wake capacity threshold — 8B model sustains 0.92 recall up to 13 edits, then CRASHES to 0.57 at 14. Sharp phase transition, not gradual decay.
ALIGNMENT TAX: RLHF suppresses LoRA-injected knowledge at scale. Inverse scaling: 3B=47%, 8B=37%, 70B=0% recall. Led them to use LoRA only during sleep.
SLEEP CONVERGENCE: 30 facts at 40% initial recall recover to 100% within 4 sleep cycles. 70B converges 2x faster.
UNBOUNDED CAPACITY: Once facts transfer to LoRA, MEMIT edits dissolve — clearing buffer for new memories. Effective lifetime capacity unbounded.
RUNS ON: MacBook Air M3 8GB (3B 4-bit), Mac Studio M4 Ultra (70B), 2x H100 (8B BF16).

backlog.title: NotebookLM Deep Learning Method (Podcast Prep)
backlog.description: Compress a semester of learning into 48 hours. Upload massive corpus → extract mental models → map expert disagreements → generate deep-understanding questions → drill with source material.

notes.text: USE THIS BEFORE LEVIN/SCHLEISMAN PODCAST
STEP 1: Upload massive corpus — textbooks, research papers, lecture transcripts, everything on the subject.
STEP 2: Ask 'What are the 5 core mental models that every expert in this field shares?' — Not 'summarize', not 'explain'. Mental models.
STEP 3: Ask 'Show me the 3 places where experts fundamentally disagree, and what each side's strongest argument is.' — Maps the intellectual landscape in 20 min.
STEP 4: Ask 'Generate 10 questions that would expose whether someone deeply understands this subject vs someone who just memorized facts.'
STEP 5: Spend hours answering those questions using source material. Every wrong answer → 'Explain why this is wrong and what I'm missing.'
KEY INSIGHT: The difference between a semester and 48 hours isn't the amount of content — it's knowing which questions to ask.
SOURCE: MIT grad student passed qualifying exam on subject he'd never studied using this exact method.

backlog.title: Memory as Reasoning (Plastic Labs)
backlog.description: Treat memory as dynamic reasoning task, not static storage. Humans use prediction & surprisal due to resource constraints — LLMs don't have these limits, so we can exceed human memory performance.

notes.text: FOR MICHAEL LEVIN PODCAST PREP — concepts about AI agent memory.
KEY INSIGHT: Memory isn't just storage — it's making predictions from incomplete data + checking for surprisal/errors to improve next predictions.
Human brains evolved under energy/information/computation constraints → prediction-based memory systems.
LLMs don't have these constraints → can reason from first principles about social information, form better representations.
Social cognition: Models of other people are constantly updated based on predictive fidelity or novelty (surprisal).
Traditional storage approaches are brittle, deal poorly with contradictions/incomplete info. Dynamic reasoning is better.
Chain-of-thought prompting ('let's think step by step') increases performance by expending more tokens reasoning.

backlog.title: Fractals: Recursive Agent Swarm Orchestrator
backlog.description: TinyAGI's recursive task orchestrator. Give it any high-level task → grows self-similar tree of subtasks → runs each leaf in isolated git worktrees with agent swarm. Two-phase: Plan then Execute.

notes.text: Architecture: OpenAI (gpt-5.2) for classify/decompose, Claude CLI for execution in isolated git worktrees.
Two-phase flow: 1) PLAN - recursive decomposition into tree, 2) EXECUTE - batch leaf tasks in worktrees.
Batch strategies: depth-first (implemented), breadth-first (roadmap), layer-sequential (roadmap).
Web UI: Task input → Tree visualization → Workspace setup → Execution status polling.
DIRECTLY RELEVANT TO OPENORCA - similar vision of recursive multi-agent orchestration with task decomposition.

backlog.title: System Prompts & Models of AI Tools (30,000+ lines)
backlog.description: Massive collection of system prompts from: Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, Windsurf, Replit, v0, Lovable, Manus, Perplexity, Warp, Notion AI, and 20+ more. Gold for understanding how top AI tools are built.

notes.text: 30,000+ lines of system prompts, internal tools, and model configs from major AI tools.
Includes: Augment Code, Claude Code, Cursor, Devin AI, Junie, Kiro, Lovable, Manus, NotionAI, Perplexity, Replit, Windsurf, v0, and many more.
Valuable for: understanding agent architectures, prompt engineering patterns, tool design decisions.
Related: Kilo Code is open-source with full system prompt + architecture visible.

backlog.title: The Only Thing That Survives (Defensibility Essay)
backlog.description: Sid's essay on startup defensibility. Build time has collapsed — what took 8 engineers 4 months can be done by 1 person in a weekend. The question: what can't be replicated by a team with $50M in 3 years?

notes.text: 7 REAL SOURCES OF DEFENSIBILITY:
1. Proprietary data that improves with use — every interaction makes product better in ways competitors can't shortcut
2. Trust at infrastructure level — become embedded in how people make decisions, move money, run operations
3. Permission moat — regulatory licenses, capital requirements, security reviews, procurement contracts
4. Distribution that can't be copied with code — being the default integration, preinstalled, in procurement catalogs
5. Community and brand independent of product — users build workflows/businesses on top, ecosystem self-perpetuates
6. Accumulated capital and liquidity — loss history, underwriting intelligence, scar tissue from real operations
7. Physical infrastructure — atoms remain stubbornly hard to copy
KEY TEST: 'If a team with $50M cloned us tomorrow, what would they still not be able to replicate in 3 years?'
RELEVANT TO OPENORCA: What's the compounding advantage? Data from agent deployments? Trust/infrastructure position? Community of builders?

backlog.title: Research: GitNexus (Code Knowledge Graph + MCP)
backlog.description: Zero-server code intelligence engine. Indexes codebases into knowledge graphs with every dependency, call chain, cluster, and execution flow. MCP integration for Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf. CLI + Web UI.

notes.text: We used GitNexus web UI before to visualize memory as knowledge graph (59 nodes, 502 edges).
Full repo has CLI + MCP integration: npm install -g gitnexus, then gitnexus analyze to index a repo.
MCP gives AI agents deep codebase awareness — supports Claude Code (full: MCP + skills + hooks), Cursor, Windsurf, OpenCode.
Like DeepWiki but deeper — tracks every relationship, not just descriptions. Could be useful for OpenOrca agent context.

backlog.title: OpenOrca: Cynefin-Based Human Escalation
backlog.description: Agents need domain awareness — ability to classify situations and know when to escalate to humans. Clear domain = handle autonomously. Complex/Chaotic = ping human, raise flag, don't decide alone.

notes.text: Ian shared LinkedIn article on Cynefin framework — maps where machines excel vs where humans are essential.
Clear domain: agents handle autonomously (rules, automation). Complicated: agents assist, humans decide. Complex/Chaotic: agents must escalate to humans.
Ian's direction: 'I need the agents to know when to ping a human or raise a flag for a human.' This is a core OpenOrca design principle.
Design questions: How does an agent detect which domain it's in? What signals indicate complexity/chaos? What's the escalation protocol?

backlog.title: Research: Headroom (Context Optimization)
backlog.description: Context compression layer for LLM apps. 70-95% token reduction on tool outputs. Includes 'failure learning' that analyzes past sessions and writes learnings to CLAUDE.md/MEMORY.md.

notes.text: Ian shared this right after we built the Kanban board. Marked high priority for OpenOrca context management.
Key features: proxy mode (zero code changes), failure learning (analyzes past Claude sessions), 87.6% token savings with same accuracy in benchmarks.

backlog.title: Research: AgentCard (Prepaid Visa for AI Agents)
backlog.description: Virtual Visa cards designed for AI agents. CLI tool with MCP integration for Claude. Single-use cards with scoped spending limits. Free during beta.

notes.text: Ian originally mentioned a prepaid Visa app but link wasn't in memory. Couldn't find it at the time.
Ian found the link: agentcard.sh — prepaid virtual Visa cards specifically for AI agents.
Key features: npm install -g agent-cards, MCP integration (agent-cards setup-mcp), single-use cards locked to loaded amount, AES-256-GCM encryption, works anywhere Visa accepted.
Highly relevant for OpenOrca — gives agents ability to make real purchases with scoped spending limits per agent/task.

backlog.title: Set Up Real Email Sending

notes.text: Tried automating ProtonMail web UI but hit sandboxed iframe security restrictions — can set To/Subject but body doesn't persist.
Nodemailer SMTP works with Ethereal (test service). For real sending need: Gmail App Password (free, quick), ProtonMail Bridge ($5/mo), or Resend/SendGrid API.

done.title: Memory Graph Visualization
done.description: Visualized memory as knowledge graph using GitNexus + md-to-js converter. 59 nodes, 502 edges.

notes.text: GitNexus is built for code (imports/function calls), not markdown. Created tools/md-to-js.js converter to transform .md files into .js modules with require() calls.
Result: 59 nodes, 502 edges — my brain visualized as a neural network. Reusable pattern for any document format.
